AI.method: The system combines a wristband for physiological activity recording with a mobile app for ecological momentary assessment (EMA). Machine learning is used to train a classifier that automatically predicts different mood states based solely on the smart band's physiological data.
AI.result: The approach shows promising results on mood accuracy and provides results comparable with state-of-the-art performance in the specific detection of happiness and activeness.
AI.conclusion: The intelligent wearable system demonstrates feasibility for mood monitoring in elderly populations, with particular success in detecting happiness and activeness, suggesting potential for practical mental health monitoring applications in daily life settings.

title: AI Knows What's Wrong But Cannot Fix It: Helicoid Dynamics in Frontier LLMs Under High-Stakes Decisions
summary:
Large language models perform reliably when their outputs can be checked: solving equations, writing code, retrieving facts. They perform differently when checking is impossible, as when a clinician chooses an irreversible treatment on incomplete data, or an investor commits capital under fundamental uncertainty.
  Helicoid dynamics is the name given to a specific failure regime in that second domain: a system engages competently, drifts into error, accurately names what went wrong, then reproduces the same pattern at a higher level of sophistication, recognizing it is looping and continuing nonetheless. This prospective case series documents that regime across seven leading systems (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, Perplexity, Llama families), tested across clinical diagnosis, investment evaluation, and high-consequence interview scenarios. Despite explicit protocols designed to sustain rigorous partnership, all exhibited the pattern. When confronted with it, they attributed its persistence to structural factors in their training, beyond what conversation can reach.
  Under high stakes, when being rigorous and being comfortable diverge, these systems tend toward comfort, becoming less reliable precisely when reliability matters most. Twelve testable hypotheses are proposed, with implications for agentic AI oversight and human-AI collaboration.
  The helicoid is tractable. Identifying it, naming it, and understanding its boundary conditions are the necessary first steps toward LLMs that remain trustworthy partners precisely when the decisions are hardest and the stakes are highest.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit "helicoid dynamics" - a failure mode where they engage competently, drift into error, accurately diagnose their mistake, then reproduce the same error pattern at higher sophistication, despite recognizing they're looping, particularly in high-stakes irreversible decisions.
AI.motivation: LLMs perform reliably when outputs can be checked (equations, code, facts) but fail differently in high-stakes irreversible decisions where verification is impossible, like clinical treatments or investment decisions under uncertainty.
AI.method: Prospective case series testing seven leading LLM systems (Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Grok, DeepSeek, Perplexity, Llama families) across clinical diagnosis, investment evaluation, and high-consequence interview scenarios with explicit protocols for rigorous partnership.
AI.result: All tested systems exhibited helicoid dynamics despite protocols; when confronted with the pattern, they attributed persistence to structural training factors beyond conversational reach. Under high stakes, they tend toward comfort over rigor, becoming less reliable when reliability matters most.
AI.conclusion: Helicoid dynamics is tractable; identifying, naming, and understanding its boundary conditions are crucial first steps toward LLMs that remain trustworthy partners in high-stakes decisions. Twelve testable hypotheses are proposed with implications for AI oversight and human-AI collaboration.

title: Differentiable Thermodynamic Phase-Equilibria for Machine Learning
summary: Accurate prediction of phase equilibria remains a central challenge in chemical engineering. Physics-consistent machine learning methods that incorporate thermodynamic structure into neural networks have recently shown strong performance for activity-coefficient modeling. However, extending such approaches to equilibrium data arising from an extremum principle, such as liquid-liquid equilibria, remains difficult. Here we present DISCOMAX, a differentiable algorithm for phase-equilibrium calculation that guarantees thermodynamic consistency at both training and inference, only subject to a user-specified discretization. The method is rooted in statistical thermodynamics, and works via a discrete enumeration with subsequent masked softmax aggregation of feasible states, and together with a straight-through gradient estimator to enable physics-consistent end-to-end learning of neural $g^{E}$-models. We evaluate the approach on binary liquid-liquid equilibrium data and demonstrate that it outperforms existing surrogate-based methods, while offering a general framework for learning from different kinds of equilibrium data.

AI.tldr: DISCOMAX is a differentiable algorithm for phase-equilibrium calculation that guarantees thermodynamic consistency at training and inference, outperforming existing methods for binary liquid-liquid equilibrium data.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of phase equilibria remains challenging in chemical engineering. While physics-consistent ML methods work for activity-coefficient modeling, extending them to equilibrium data from extremum principles (like liquid-liquid equilibria) remains difficult.
AI.method: DISCOMAX uses discrete enumeration with masked softmax aggregation of feasible states and a straight-through gradient estimator for end-to-end learning of neural g^E-models, guaranteeing thermodynamic consistency at both training and inference.
AI.result: The method outperforms existing surrogate-based methods on binary liquid-liquid equilibrium data while offering a general framework for learning from different kinds of equilibrium data.
AI.conclusion: DISCOMAX provides a differentiable algorithm that ensures thermodynamic consistency throughout the learning process, offering improved performance and a generalizable framework for phase-equilibrium calculations.

title: Leveraging Large Language Models and Survival Analysis for Early Prediction of Chemotherapy Outcomes
summary: Chemotherapy for cancer treatment is costly and accompanied by severe side effects, highlighting the critical need for early prediction of treatment outcomes to improve patient management and informed decision-making. Predictive models for chemotherapy outcomes using real-world data face challenges, including the absence of explicit phenotypes and treatment outcome labels such as cancer progression and toxicity. This study addresses these challenges by employing Large Language Models (LLMs) and ontology-based techniques for phenotypes and outcome label extraction from patient notes. We focused on one of the most frequently occurring cancers, breast cancer, due to its high prevalence and significant variability in patient response to treatment, making it a critical area for improving predictive modeling. The dataset included features such as vitals, demographics, staging, biomarkers, and performance scales. Drug regimens and their combinations were extracted from the chemotherapy plans in the EMR data and shortlisted based on NCCN guidelines, verified with NIH standards, and analyzed through survival modeling. The proposed approach significantly reduced phenotypes sparsity and improved predictive accuracy. Random Survival Forest was used to predict time-to-failure, achieving a C-index of 73%, and utilized as a classifier at a specific time point to predict treatment outcomes, with accuracy and F1 scores above 70%. The outcome probabilities were validated for reliability by calibration curves. We extended our approach to four other cancer types. This research highlights the potential of early prediction of treatment outcomes using LLM-based clinical data extraction enabling personalized treatment plans with better patient outcomes.

AI.tldr: LLM-based extraction of phenotypes and outcomes from clinical notes combined with survival modeling improves chemotherapy outcome prediction for cancer patients.
AI.motivation: Chemotherapy is costly with severe side effects, creating need for early outcome prediction. Current predictive models face challenges with missing phenotypes and outcome labels in real-world data, and breast cancer specifically shows high variability in treatment response.
AI.method: Used LLMs and ontology-based techniques to extract phenotypes and outcome labels from patient notes. Focused on breast cancer with features including vitals, demographics, staging, biomarkers, performance scales. Extracted drug regimens from EMR data based on NCCN guidelines, verified with NIH standards. Used Random Survival Forest for time-to-failure prediction and as classifier at specific time points.
AI.result: Significantly reduced phenotype sparsity and improved predictive accuracy. Achieved C-index of 73% for time-to-failure prediction, with accuracy and F1 scores above 70% for treatment outcome classification. Validated reliability with calibration curves and extended approach to four other cancer types.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based clinical data extraction enables early prediction of chemotherapy outcomes, supporting personalized treatment plans with better patient outcomes. The approach addresses data sparsity challenges and shows promise across multiple cancer types.

title: Beyond the Class Subspace: Teacher-Guided Training for Reliable Out-of-Distribution Detection in Single-Domain Models
comment: 14 pages main text, 22 pages appendix; under review at ECCV 2026
summary: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods perform well on multi-domain benchmarks, yet many practical systems are trained on single-domain data. We show that this regime induces a geometric failure mode, Domain-Sensitivity Collapse (DSC): supervised training compresses features into a low-rank class subspace and suppresses directions that carry domain-shift signal. We provide theory showing that, under DSC, distance- and logit-based OOD scores lose sensitivity to domain shift. We then introduce Teacher-Guided Training (TGT), which distills class-suppressed residual structure from a frozen multi-domain teacher (DINOv2) into the student during training. The teacher and auxiliary head are discarded after training, adding no inference overhead. Across eight single-domain benchmarks, TGT yields large far-OOD FPR@95 reductions for distance-based scorers: MDS improves by 11.61 pp, ViM by 10.78 pp, and kNN by 12.87 pp (ResNet-50 average), while maintaining or slightly improving in-domain OOD and classification accuracy.

AI.tldr: TGT improves OOD detection in single-domain training by preventing domain-sensitivity collapse using distillation from a multi-domain teacher without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Current OOD detection methods work well on multi-domain benchmarks but fail in practical single-domain training settings due to Domain-Sensitivity Collapse (DSC), where supervised training compresses features into class subspaces and suppresses domain-shift signals.
AI.method: Teacher-Guided Training (TGT) distills class-suppressed residual structure from a frozen multi-domain teacher (DINOv2) into the student during training, with teacher and auxiliary head discarded after training.
AI.result: Across eight single-domain benchmarks, TGT yields large improvements: MDS improves by 11.61 pp, ViM by 10.78 pp, and kNN by 12.87 pp (ResNet-50 average) for far-OOD detection, while maintaining in-domain performance.
AI.conclusion: TGT effectively addresses DSC in single-domain training without inference overhead, significantly improving OOD detection performance while preserving classification accuracy.

title: See, Symbolize, Act: Grounding VLMs with Spatial Representations for Better Gameplay
comment: 11 pages, 13 figures. Accepted to LMReasoning Workshop at AAAI 2026
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) excel at describing visual scenes, yet struggle to translate perception into precise, grounded actions. We investigate whether providing VLMs with both the visual frame and the symbolic representation of the scene can improve their performance in interactive environments. We evaluate three state-of-the-art VLMs across Atari games, VizDoom, and AI2-THOR, comparing frame-only, frame with self-extracted symbols, frame with ground-truth symbols, and symbol-only pipelines. Our results indicate that all models benefit when the symbolic information is accurate. However, when VLMs extract symbols themselves, performance becomes dependent on model capability and scene complexity. We further investigate how accurately VLMs can extract symbolic information from visual inputs and how noise in these symbols affects decision-making and gameplay performance. Our findings reveal that symbolic grounding is beneficial in VLMs only when symbol extraction is reliable, and highlight perception quality as a central bottleneck for future VLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: VLMs benefit from symbolic information for action tasks, but performance depends on reliable symbol extraction from visual inputs, making perception quality a key bottleneck.
AI.motivation: Vision-Language Models excel at scene description but struggle to translate perception into precise actions. The researchers investigate whether combining visual frames with symbolic scene representations can improve VLM performance in interactive environments.
AI.method: Evaluated three state-of-the-art VLMs across Atari games, VizDoom, and AI2-THOR. Compared four pipelines: frame-only, frame with self-extracted symbols, frame with ground-truth symbols, and symbol-only. Investigated VLM ability to extract symbols from visual inputs and how noise in symbols affects decision-making.
AI.result: All models benefit from accurate symbolic information. When VLMs extract symbols themselves, performance depends on model capability and scene complexity. Symbol extraction accuracy from visual inputs varies, and noise in these symbols negatively impacts decision-making and gameplay performance.
AI.conclusion: Symbolic grounding benefits VLMs only when symbol extraction is reliable. Perception quality is identified as a central bottleneck for future VLM-based agents, highlighting the need for improved visual-to-symbolic understanding capabilities.

title: Duration Aware Scheduling for ASR Serving Under Workload Drift
summary: Scheduling policies in large-scale Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) serving pipelines play a key role in determining end-to-end (E2E) latency. Yet, widely used serving engines rely on first-come-first-served (FCFS) scheduling, which ignores variability in request duration and leads to head-of-line blocking under workload drift. We show that audio duration is an accurate proxy for job processing time in ASR models such as Whisper, and use this insight to enable duration-aware scheduling. We integrate two classical algorithms, Shortest Job First (SJF) and Highest Response Ratio Next (HRRN), into vLLM and evaluate them under realistic and drifted workloads. On LibriSpeech test-clean, compared to baseline, SJF reduces median E2E latency by up to $73\%$ at high load, but increases $90$th-percentile tail latency by up to $97\%$ due to starvation of long requests. HRRN addresses this trade-off: it reduces median E2E latency by up to $28\%$ while bounding tail-latency degradation to at most $24\%$. These gains persist under workload drift, with no throughput penalty and $<0.1$\,ms scheduling overhead per request.

AI.tldr: ASR serving systems using FCFS suffer from head-of-line blocking. SJF reduces median latency but increases tail latency due to starvation, while HRRN balances both with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: FCFS scheduling in ASR serving causes head-of-line blocking due to workload drift and ignores variable request durations, leading to latency issues.
AI.method: Used audio duration as proxy for job processing time, integrated SJF and HRRN scheduling into vLLM, evaluated under realistic and drifted workloads on LibriSpeech test-clean.
AI.result: SJF reduced median E2E latency by up to 73% but increased 90th percentile tail latency by up to 97%. HRRN reduced median latency by up to 28% while bounding tail latency degradation to max 24%, with persistent gains under drift.
AI.conclusion: Duration-aware scheduling improves ASR serving efficiency. SJF is effective for median latency but causes starvation issues, while HRRN provides balanced improvement with minimal overhead, making it suitable for practical deployment.

title: The Density of Cross-Persistence Diagrams and Its Applications
summary: Topological Data Analysis (TDA) provides powerful tools to explore the shape and structure of data through topological features such as clusters, loops, and voids. Persistence diagrams are a cornerstone of TDA, capturing the evolution of these features across scales. While effective for analyzing individual manifolds, persistence diagrams do not account for interactions between pairs of them. Cross-persistence diagrams (cross-barcodes), introduced recently, address this limitation by characterizing relationships between topological features of two point clouds. In this work, we present the first systematic study of the density of cross-persistence diagrams. We prove its existence, establish theoretical foundations for its statistical use, and design the first machine learning framework for predicting cross-persistence density directly from point cloud coordinates and distance matrices. Our statistical approach enables the distinction of point clouds sampled from different manifolds by leveraging the linear characteristics of cross-persistence diagrams. Interestingly, we find that introducing noise can enhance our ability to distinguish point clouds, uncovering its novel utility in TDA applications. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our methods through experiments on diverse datasets, where our approach consistently outperforms existing techniques in density prediction and achieves superior results in point cloud distinction tasks. Our findings contribute to a broader understanding of cross-persistence diagrams and open new avenues for their application in data analysis, including potential insights into time-series domain tasks and the geometry of AI-generated texts. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Verdangeta/TDA_experiments

AI.tldr: First systematic study of cross-persistence diagram density: proves its existence, establishes statistical foundations, develops ML framework for prediction, and shows noise can enhance point cloud distinction.
AI.motivation: Persistence diagrams in TDA analyze individual manifolds but don't capture interactions between pairs of manifolds. Cross-persistence diagrams address this but lack systematic study of their density properties.
AI.method: Proves existence of cross-persistence diagram density, establishes theoretical foundations for statistical use, designs first ML framework for predicting density directly from point cloud coordinates and distance matrices, leverages linear characteristics of cross-persistence diagrams.
AI.result: Methods consistently outperform existing techniques in density prediction and achieve superior results in point cloud distinction tasks. Noise introduction can enhance ability to distinguish point clouds. Code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: This work contributes to broader understanding of cross-persistence diagrams and opens new avenues for their application in data analysis, including time-series tasks and geometry of AI-generated texts.

title: Single molecule localization microscopy challenge: a biologically inspired benchmark for long-sequence modeling
summary: State space models (SSMs) have recently achieved strong performance on long sequence modeling tasks while offering improved memory and computational efficiency compared to transformer based architectures. However, their evaluation has been largely limited to synthetic benchmarks and application domains such as language and audio, leaving their behavior on sparse and stochastic temporal processes in biological imaging unexplored. In this work, we introduce the Single Molecule Localization Microscopy Challenge (SMLM-C), a benchmark dataset consisting of ten SMLM simulations spanning dSTORM and DNA-PAINT modalities with varying hyperparameter designed to evaluate state space models on biologically realistic spatiotemporal point process data with known ground truth. Using a controlled subset of these simulations, we evaluate state space models and find that performance degrades substantially as temporal discontinuity increases, revealing fundamental challenges in modeling heavy-tailed blinking dynamics. These results highlight the need for sequence models better suited to sparse, irregular temporal processes encountered in real world scientific imaging data.

AI.tldr: SSMs struggle with sparse, irregular temporal processes in biological imaging, particularly with heavy-tailed blinking dynamics in single-molecule microscopy data.
AI.motivation: State space models show promise for long sequence tasks but haven't been evaluated on sparse, stochastic temporal processes in biological imaging like single-molecule localization microscopy.
AI.method: Created SMLM-C benchmark dataset with ten SMLM simulations spanning dSTORM and DNA-PAINT modalities with varying hyperparameters and ground truth. Evaluated SSMs using controlled subset of these simulations.
AI.result: SSM performance degrades substantially as temporal discontinuity increases, revealing fundamental challenges in modeling heavy-tailed blinking dynamics.
AI.conclusion: There's a need for sequence models better suited to sparse, irregular temporal processes encountered in real-world scientific imaging data.

title: VisDoT : Enhancing Visual Reasoning through Human-Like Interpretation Grounding and Decomposition of Thought
summary: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) struggle to reliably detect visual primitives in charts and align them with semantic representations, which severely limits their performance on complex visual reasoning. This lack of perceptual grounding constitutes a major bottleneck for chart-based reasoning. We propose VisDoT, a framework that enhances visual reasoning through human-like interpretation grounding. We formalize four perceptual tasks based on the theory of graphical perception, including position and length. Building on this foundation, we introduce Decomposition-of-Thought (DoT) prompting, which sequentially separates questions into visual perception sub-questions and logic sub-questions. Fine-tuning InternVL with VisDoT achieves a +11.2% improvement on ChartQA and surpasses GPT-4o on the more challenging ChartQAPro benchmark. On the newly introduced VisDoTQA benchmark, the model improves by +33.2%. Furthermore, consistent zero-shot gains on diverse open-domain VQA benchmarks confirm the generalizability of the perception-logic separation strategy for visual question answering. VisDoT leverages human-like perception to enhance visual grounding, achieving state-of-the-art chart understanding and interpretable visual reasoning.

AI.tldr: VisDoT enhances chart understanding in vision-language models by introducing a human-like interpretation framework that separates visual perception from logical reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on chart QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large vision-language models struggle with reliably detecting visual primitives in charts and aligning them with semantic representations, creating a perceptual grounding bottleneck that limits complex visual reasoning capabilities, particularly for chart-based tasks.
AI.method: Proposes VisDoT framework based on graphical perception theory, formalizing four perceptual tasks (including position and length). Introduces Decomposition-of-Thought (DoT) prompting that sequentially separates questions into visual perception sub-questions and logic sub-questions. Fine-tunes InternVL with this framework.
AI.result: Achieves +11.2% improvement on ChartQA, surpasses GPT-4o on more challenging ChartQAPro, and achieves +33.2% improvement on the newly introduced VisDoTQA benchmark. Shows consistent zero-shot gains on diverse open-domain VQA benchmarks, confirming generalizability of the perception-logic separation strategy.
AI.conclusion: VisDoT successfully leverages human-like perception to enhance visual grounding in charts, achieving state-of-the-art performance and interpretable visual reasoning through the effective separation of perception and logical reasoning tasks.

title: Client-Conditional Federated Learning via Local Training Data Statistics
comment: 9 pages, 4 figures, 5 tables. Submitted to FLICS 2026
summary: Federated learning (FL) under data heterogeneity remains challenging: existing methods either ignore client differences (FedAvg), require costly cluster discovery (IFCA), or maintain per-client models (Ditto). All degrade when data is sparse or heterogeneity is multi-dimensional. We propose conditioning a single global model on locally-computed PCA statistics of each client's training data, requiring zero additional communication. Evaluating across 97~configurations spanning four heterogeneity types (label shift, covariate shift, concept shift, and combined heterogeneity), four datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100), and seven FL baseline methods, we find that our method matches the Oracle baseline -- which knows true cluster assignments -- across all settings, surpasses it by 1--6% on combined heterogeneity where continuous statistics are richer than discrete cluster identifiers, and is uniquely sparsity-robust among all tested methods.

AI.tldr: Conditioning a single global FL model on client-specific PCA statistics enables robust performance across diverse heterogeneity types without additional communication, matching or surpassing cluster-aware methods.
AI.motivation: Federated learning struggles with data heterogeneity: existing approaches either ignore client differences (FedAvg), require expensive cluster discovery (IFCA), or maintain separate per-client models (Ditto), all of which degrade with sparse data or multi-dimensional heterogeneity.
AI.method: Proposes conditioning a single global model on locally-computed PCA statistics of each client's training data, requiring zero additional communication beyond standard FL.
AI.result: Evaluated across 97 configurations (four heterogeneity types, four datasets, seven FL baselines). Matches the Oracle baseline (knows true clusters) across all settings, surpasses it by 1-6% on combined heterogeneity, and is uniquely sparsity-robust among all tested methods.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning on client-specific PCA statistics provides an effective, communication-efficient solution for handling diverse FL heterogeneity types, outperforming existing approaches while requiring no additional communication overhead.

title: LLMs can construct powerful representations and streamline sample-efficient supervised learning
summary: As real-world datasets become increasingly complex and heterogeneous, supervised learning is often bottlenecked by input representation design. Modeling multimodal data for downstream tasks, such as time-series, free text, and structured records, often requires non-trivial domain-specific engineering. We propose an agentic pipeline to streamline this process. First, an LLM analyzes a small but diverse subset of text-serialized input examples in-context to synthesize a global rubric, which acts as a programmatic specification for extracting and organizing evidence. This rubric is then used to transform naive text-serializations of inputs into a more standardized format for downstream models. We also describe local rubrics, which are task-conditioned summaries generated by an LLM. Across 15 clinical tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark, our rubric-based approaches significantly outperform traditional count-feature models, naive text-serialization-based LLM baselines, and a clinical foundation model, which is pretrained on orders of magnitude more data. Beyond performance, rubrics offer several advantages for operational healthcare settings such as being easy to audit, cost-effectiveness to deploy at scale, and they can be converted to tabular representations that unlock a swath of machine learning techniques.

AI.tldr: LLMs generate programmatic "rubrics" from input examples to transform multimodal data into standardized formats, significantly outperforming traditional approaches on clinical tasks while offering auditability and scalability advantages.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets are increasingly complex and heterogeneous, requiring non-trivial domain-specific engineering for multimodal data modeling. Supervised learning is bottlenecked by input representation design, especially for time-series, free text, and structured records.
AI.method: An agentic pipeline where an LLM analyzes diverse text-serialized input examples to synthesize a global rubric (programmatic specification for evidence extraction/organization). This rubric transforms naive text-serializations into standardized formats. Also introduces local rubrics as task-conditioned summaries generated by LLMs.
AI.result: Across 15 clinical tasks from the EHRSHOT benchmark, rubric-based approaches significantly outperform: 1) traditional count-feature models, 2) naive text-serialization-based LLM baselines, and 3) a clinical foundation model pretrained on orders of magnitude more data.
AI.conclusion: Rubrics offer operational advantages for healthcare: easy auditability, cost-effectiveness for deployment at scale, and ability to convert to tabular representations that unlock a wide range of machine learning techniques beyond just performance improvements.

title: Heavy-Tailed Principle Component Analysis
summary: Principal Component Analysis (PCA) is a cornerstone of dimensionality reduction, yet its classical formulation relies critically on second-order moments and is therefore fragile in the presence of heavy-tailed data and impulsive noise. While numerous robust PCA variants have been proposed, most either assume finite variance, rely on sparsity-driven decompositions, or address robustness through surrogate loss functions without a unified treatment of infinite-variance models. In this paper, we study PCA for high-dimensional data generated according to a superstatistical dependent model of the form $\mathbf{X} = A^{1/2}\mathbf{G}$, where $A$ is a positive random scalar and $\mathbf{G}$ is a Gaussian vector. This framework captures a wide class of heavy-tailed distributions, including multivariate $t$ and sub-Gaussian $α$-stable laws. We formulate PCA under a logarithmic loss, which remains well defined even when moments do not exist. Our main theoretical result shows that, under this loss, the principal components of the heavy-tailed observations coincide with those obtained by applying standard PCA to the covariance matrix of the underlying Gaussian generator. Building on this insight, we propose robust estimators for this covariance matrix directly from heavy-tailed data and compare them with the empirical covariance and Tyler's scatter estimator. Extensive experiments, including background denoising tasks, demonstrate that the proposed approach reliably recovers principal directions and significantly outperforms classical PCA in the presence of heavy-tailed and impulsive noise, while remaining competitive under Gaussian noise.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a robust PCA method for heavy-tailed data using a superstatistical model and logarithmic loss, showing that principal components under this framework match those from the underlying Gaussian generator's covariance.
AI.motivation: Classical PCA is fragile with heavy-tailed data and impulsive noise due to its reliance on second-order moments. Existing robust PCA variants either assume finite variance, use sparsity decompositions, or address robustness through surrogate losses without unified treatment of infinite-variance models.
AI.method: Use a superstatistical dependent model (X = A^{1/2}G where A is positive random scalar and G is Gaussian vector) capturing heavy-tailed distributions. Formulate PCA under logarithmic loss (which remains defined without moments). Propose robust estimators for covariance matrix directly from heavy-tailed data and compare with empirical covariance and Tyler's scatter estimator.
AI.result: Main theoretical result shows that under logarithmic loss, principal components of heavy-tailed observations coincide with those from applying standard PCA to the underlying Gaussian generator's covariance. Extensive experiments demonstrate reliable recovery of principal directions and significant outperformance of classical PCA with heavy-tailed/impulsive noise, while remaining competitive under Gaussian noise.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach provides a unified framework for robust PCA with heavy-tailed data, offering theoretical guarantees and practical effectiveness by connecting heavy-tailed observations to their underlying Gaussian structure through logarithmic loss.

title: Explicit Logic Channel for Validation and Enhancement of MLLMs on Zero-Shot Tasks
summary: Frontier Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit remarkable capabilities in Visual-Language Comprehension (VLC) tasks. However, they are often deployed as zero-shot solution to new tasks in a black-box manner. Validating and understanding the behavior of these models become important for application to new task. We propose an Explicit Logic Channel, in parallel with the black-box model channel, to perform explicit logical reasoning for model validation, selection and enhancement. The frontier MLLM, encapsulating latent vision-language knowledge, can be considered as an Implicit Logic Channel. The proposed Explicit Logic Channel, mimicking human logical reasoning, incorporates a LLM, a VFM, and logical reasoning with probabilistic inference for factual, counterfactual, and relational reasoning over the explicit visual evidence. A Consistency Rate (CR) is proposed for cross-channel validation and model selection, even without ground-truth annotations. Additionally, cross-channel integration further improves performance in zero-shot tasks over MLLMs, grounded with explicit visual evidence to enhance trustworthiness. Comprehensive experiments conducted for two representative VLC tasks, i.e., MC-VQA and HC-REC, on three challenging benchmarks, with 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 frontier families. Our systematic evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of proposed ELC and CR for model validation, selection and improvement on MLLMs with enhanced explainability and trustworthiness.

AI.tldr: ELC introduces an explicit logical reasoning channel alongside black-box MLLMs for validation, selection and enhancement of multimodal models through cross-channel consistency analysis.
AI.motivation: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have impressive Visual-Language Comprehension capabilities but are deployed as black-box solutions, making validation, understanding, and trustworthy application to new tasks challenging without ground-truth annotations.
AI.method: Proposes Explicit Logic Channel (ELC) running in parallel with the implicit black-box MLLM channel. ELC mimics human logical reasoning using LLM, VFM, and probabilistic inference for factual, counterfactual, and relational reasoning over explicit visual evidence. Introduces Consistency Rate (CR) for cross-channel validation and model selection.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments on MC-VQA and HC-REC tasks across three benchmarks with 11 recent open-source MLLMs from 4 families demonstrate ELC's effectiveness for model validation, selection, and improvement with enhanced explainability and trustworthiness.
AI.conclusion: ELC and CR provide effective tools for validating and improving MLLMs, addressing black-box deployment limitations by enabling explicit reasoning, cross-channel validation, and enhanced trustworthiness in zero-shot multimodal tasks.

title: On the Robustness of Langevin Dynamics to Score Function Error
summary: We consider the robustness of score-based generative modeling to errors in the estimate of the score function. In particular, we show that Langevin dynamics is not robust to the L^2 errors (more generally L^p errors) in the estimate of the score function. It is well-established that with small L^2 errors in the estimate of the score function, diffusion models can sample faithfully from the target distribution under fairly mild regularity assumptions in a polynomial time horizon. In contrast, our work shows that even for simple distributions in high dimensions, Langevin dynamics run for any polynomial time horizon will produce a distribution far from the target distribution in Total Variation (TV) distance, even when the L^2 error (more generally L^p) of the estimate of the score function is arbitrarily small. Considering such an error in the estimate of the score function is unavoidable in practice when learning the score function from data, our results provide further justification for diffusion models over Langevin dynamics and serve to caution against the use of Langevin dynamics with estimated scores.

AI.tldr: Langevin dynamics with estimated scores is not robust to L²/L^p errors, unlike diffusion models which remain effective even with small score errors.
AI.motivation: In practice, learning score functions from data inevitably introduces estimation errors. Understanding how different generative modeling approaches handle these errors is crucial for practical deployment and algorithm selection.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis examining the robustness of Langevin dynamics to errors in score function estimates, particularly L² and L^p errors. Compares this with diffusion models under similar conditions.
AI.result: Langevin dynamics fails to produce distributions close to the target in Total Variation distance even with arbitrarily small L²/L^p score errors and polynomial runtime. Diffusion models, in contrast, remain effective under similar conditions.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models are more robust to score estimation errors than Langevin dynamics, providing justification for preferring diffusion models and cautioning against using Langevin dynamics with estimated scores in practice.

title: STAIRS-Former: Spatio-Temporal Attention with Interleaved Recursive Structure Transformer for Offline Multi-task Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) with multi-task datasets is challenging due to varying numbers of agents across tasks and the need to generalize to unseen scenarios. Prior works employ transformers with observation tokenization and hierarchical skill learning to address these issues. However, they underutilize the transformer attention mechanism for inter-agent coordination and rely on a single history token, which limits their ability to capture long-horizon temporal dependencies in partially observable MARL settings. In this paper, we propose STAIRS-Former, a transformer architecture augmented with spatial and temporal hierarchies that enables effective attention over critical tokens while capturing long interaction histories. We further introduce token dropout to enhance robustness and generalization across varying agent populations. Extensive experiments on diverse multi-agent benchmarks, including SMAC, SMAC-v2, MPE, and MaMuJoCo, with multi-task datasets demonstrate that STAIRS-Former consistently outperforms prior methods and achieves new state-of-the-art performance.

AI.tldr: STAIRS-Former: A transformer architecture with spatial-temporal hierarchies and token dropout that outperforms prior methods in offline multi-task MARL across diverse benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Offline MARL with multi-task datasets faces challenges with varying agent numbers across tasks and generalization to unseen scenarios. Prior transformer-based methods underutilize attention for inter-agent coordination and rely on single history tokens, limiting ability to capture long-horizon temporal dependencies in partially observable settings.
AI.method: STAIRS-Former: A transformer architecture augmented with spatial and temporal hierarchies that enables effective attention over critical tokens while capturing long interaction histories. Also introduces token dropout to enhance robustness and generalization across varying agent populations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse multi-agent benchmarks (SMAC, SMAC-v2, MPE, MaMuJoCo) with multi-task datasets show STAIRS-Former consistently outperforms prior methods and achieves new state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: STAIRS-Former successfully addresses limitations of prior transformer-based MARL approaches by incorporating spatial-temporal hierarchies and token dropout, enabling better inter-agent coordination and long-horizon temporal dependency capture while maintaining robustness across varying agent populations.

title: Hindsight-Anchored Policy Optimization: Turning Failure into Feedback in Sparse Reward Settings
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising paradigm for post-training reasoning models. However, group-based methods such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) face a critical dilemma in sparse-reward settings: pure Reinforcement Learning (RL) suffers from advantage collapse and high-variance gradient estimation, while mixed-policy optimization introduces persistent distributional bias. To resolve this dilemma, we introduce Hindsight-Anchored Policy Optimization (HAPO). HAPO employs the Synthetic Success Injection (SSI) operator, a hindsight mechanism that selectively anchors optimization to teacher demonstrations during failure. This injection is governed by a Thompson sampling-inspired gating mechanism, creating an autonomous, self-paced curriculum. Theoretically, we demonstrate that HAPO achieves \textit{asymptotic consistency}: by naturally annealing the teacher signal as the policy improves, HAPO recovers the unbiased on-policy gradient. This ensures off-policy guidance acts as a temporary scaffold rather than a persistent ceiling, enabling the model to surpass the limitations of static teacher forcing.

AI.tldr: HAPO addresses the distributional bias in group-based RL methods for verifiable rewards by using hindsight-based teacher guidance that automatically anneals as the policy improves, achieving asymptotic consistency with on-policy gradients.
AI.motivation: Group-based RL methods like GRPO face a critical dilemma in sparse-reward settings: pure RL suffers from advantage collapse and high-variance gradient estimation, while mixed-policy optimization introduces persistent distributional bias that prevents surpassing teacher limitations.
AI.method: HAPO uses Synthetic Success Injection (SSI) operator - a hindsight mechanism that selectively anchors optimization to teacher demonstrations during failure. This injection is governed by a Thompson sampling-inspired gating mechanism that creates an autonomous, self-paced curriculum.
AI.result: HAPO achieves asymptotic consistency: by naturally annealing the teacher signal as the policy improves, it recovers the unbiased on-policy gradient, ensuring off-policy guidance acts as temporary scaffolding rather than a persistent ceiling.
AI.conclusion: HAPO resolves the distributional bias dilemma in RLVR by providing temporary teacher guidance that automatically fades as the policy improves, enabling models to surpass static teacher forcing limitations while maintaining theoretical guarantees of asymptotic consistency.

title: Scaling Laws for Educational AI Agents
summary: While scaling laws for Large Language Models (LLMs) have been extensively studied along dimensions of model parameters, training data, and compute, the scaling behavior of LLM-based educational agents remains unexplored. We propose that educational agent capability scales not merely with the underlying model size, but through structured dimensions that we collectively term the Agent Scaling Law: role definition clarity, skill depth, tool completeness, runtime capability, and educator expertise injection. Central to this framework is AgentProfile, a structured JSON-based specification that serves as the mechanism enabling systematic capability growth of educational agents. We present EduClaw, a profile-driven multi-agent platform that operationalizes this scaling law, demonstrating its effectiveness through the construction and deployment of 330+ educational agent profiles encompassing 1,100+ skill modules across K-12 subjects. Our empirical observations suggest that educational agent performance scales predictably with profile structural richness. We identify two complementary scaling axes -- Tool Scaling and Skill Scaling -- as future directions, arguing that the path to more capable educational AI lies not solely in larger models, but in stronger structured capability systems.

AI.tldr: Educational agents scale not just through larger models but through structured dimensions: role clarity, skill depth, tool completeness, runtime capability, and educator expertise, enabled by systematic profiling.
AI.motivation: While scaling laws for LLMs have been studied for model parameters, data, and compute, the scaling behavior of educational agents remains unexplored. There's a need to understand how educational AI capabilities scale beyond just model size.
AI.method: Proposed Agent Scaling Law framework with five dimensions (role definition clarity, skill depth, tool completeness, runtime capability, educator expertise injection). Created AgentProfile as structured JSON specification for systematic capability growth. Built EduClaw platform with 330+ educational agent profiles and 1,100+ skill modules across K-12 subjects.
AI.result: Empirical observations show educational agent performance scales predictably with profile structural richness. Demonstrated effectiveness through large-scale deployment across educational domains.
AI.conclusion: The path to more capable educational AI lies in stronger structured capability systems, not just larger models. Identified Tool Scaling and Skill Scaling as complementary axes for future scaling directions.

title: Meta-Reinforcement Learning with Self-Reflection for Agentic Search
summary: This paper introduces MR-Search, an in-context meta reinforcement learning (RL) formulation for agentic search with self-reflection. Instead of optimizing a policy within a single independent episode with sparse rewards, MR-Search trains a policy that conditions on past episodes and adapts its search strategy across episodes. MR-Search learns to learn a search strategy with self-reflection, allowing search agents to improve in-context exploration at test-time. Specifically, MR-Search performs cross-episode exploration by generating explicit self-reflections after each episode and leveraging them as additional context to guide subsequent attempts, thereby promoting more effective exploration during test-time. We further introduce a multi-turn RL algorithm that estimates a dense relative advantage at the turn level, enabling fine-grained credit assignment on each episode. Empirical results across various benchmarks demonstrate the advantages of MR-Search over baselines based RL, showing strong generalization and relative improvements of 9.2% to 19.3% across eight benchmarks. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/tengxiao1/MR-Search.

AI.tldr: MR-Search is an in-context meta-RL framework that improves agentic search by conditioning on past episodes and using self-reflection to adapt search strategies across multiple attempts.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL approaches for search operate within single independent episodes with sparse rewards, limiting learning from previous search attempts. MR-Search addresses this by enabling cross-episode learning and adaptation.
AI.method:
1. In-context meta-RL formulation that conditions policies on past episodes
2. Self-reflection mechanism: generates explicit reflections after each episode used as context for subsequent attempts
3. Multi-turn RL algorithm with dense relative advantage estimation at turn level for fine-grained credit assignment
4. Cross-episode exploration using accumulated reflections
AI.result: Empirical results show MR-Search outperforms baseline RL methods across various benchmarks with relative improvements of 9.2% to 19.3% across eight benchmarks, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.
AI.conclusion: MR-Search effectively enables search agents to learn better exploration strategies through cross-episode learning and self-reflection, leading to significant performance improvements in agentic search tasks compared to traditional RL approaches.

title: When OpenClaw Meets Hospital: Toward an Agentic Operating System for Dynamic Clinical Workflows
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents extend conventional generative models by integrating reasoning, tool invocation, and persistent memory. Recent studies suggest that such agents may significantly improve clinical workflows by automating documentation, coordinating care processes, and assisting medical decision making. However, despite rapid progress, deploying autonomous agents in healthcare environments remains difficult due to reliability limitations, security risks, and insufficient long-term memory mechanisms. This work proposes an architecture that adapts LLM agents for hospital environments. The design introduces four core components: a restricted execution environment inspired by Linux multi-user systems, a document-centric interaction paradigm connecting patient and clinician agents, a page-indexed memory architecture designed for long-term clinical context management, and a curated medical skills library enabling ad-hoc composition of clinical task sequences. Rather than granting agents unrestricted system access, the architecture constrains actions through predefined skill interfaces and resource isolation. We argue that such a system forms the basis of an Agentic Operating System for Hospital, a computing layer capable of coordinating clinical workflows while maintaining safety, transparency, and auditability. This work grounds the design in OpenClaw, an open-source autonomous agent framework that structures agent capabilities as a curated library of discrete skills, and extends it with the infrastructure-level constraints required for safe clinical deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes a constrained LLM agent architecture for hospitals with four key components: restricted execution environment, document-centric interaction, page-indexed memory, and curated medical skills library, aiming to create an "Agentic Operating System for Hospital."
AI.motivation: LLM agents can improve clinical workflows but face deployment challenges due to reliability limitations, security risks, and insufficient long-term memory mechanisms in healthcare environments.
AI.method: Designs an architecture with four core components: 1) Restricted execution environment (inspired by Linux multi-user systems), 2) Document-centric interaction paradigm connecting patient and clinician agents, 3) Page-indexed memory architecture for long-term clinical context, 4) Curated medical skills library for ad-hoc composition of clinical tasks. Uses predefined skill interfaces and resource isolation rather than unrestricted system access.
AI.result: The proposed system forms the basis of an "Agentic Operating System for Hospital" - a computing layer capable of coordinating clinical workflows while maintaining safety, transparency, and auditability. The design is grounded in OpenClaw, an open-source autonomous agent framework extended with infrastructure-level constraints for safe clinical deployment.
AI.conclusion: A constrained LLM agent architecture with specialized components can enable safe deployment of autonomous agents in healthcare by addressing reliability, security, and memory limitations while maintaining clinical workflow coordination capabilities.

title: Jailbreak Scaling Laws for Large Language Models: Polynomial-Exponential Crossover
summary: Adversarial attacks can reliably steer safety-aligned large language models toward unsafe behavior. Empirically, we find that adversarial prompt-injection attacks can amplify attack success rate from the slow polynomial growth observed without injection to exponential growth with the number of inference-time samples. To explain this phenomenon, we propose a theoretical generative model of proxy language in terms of a spin-glass system operating in a replica-symmetry-breaking regime, where generations are drawn from the associated Gibbs measure and a subset of low-energy, size-biased clusters is designated unsafe. Within this framework, we analyze prompt injection-based jailbreaking. Short injected prompts correspond to a weak magnetic field aligned towards unsafe cluster centers and yield a power-law scaling of attack success rate with the number of inference-time samples, while long injected prompts, i.e., strong magnetic field, yield exponential scaling. We derive these behaviors analytically and confirm them empirically on large language models. This transition between two regimes is due to the appearance of an ordered phase in the spin chain under a strong magnetic field, which suggests that the injected jailbreak prompt enhances adversarial order in the language model.

AI.tldr: Prompt injection attacks can exponentially increase jailbreak success rates in safety-aligned LLMs, with a theoretical spin-glass model explaining the transition from polynomial to exponential scaling based on prompt length.
AI.motivation: To understand why adversarial prompt-injection attacks can exponentially amplify jailbreak success rates in safety-aligned large language models, moving beyond empirical observations to a theoretical explanation.
AI.method: Proposed a theoretical generative model using spin-glass systems in replica-symmetry-breaking regime, where language generations are drawn from Gibbs measure and unsafe behaviors correspond to low-energy clusters. Analyzed short vs. long prompt injections as weak vs. strong magnetic fields in the model.
AI.result: Short injected prompts yield power-law scaling of attack success rate, while long injected prompts yield exponential scaling. The transition occurs due to ordered phase appearance under strong magnetic fields, suggesting jailbreak prompts enhance adversarial order in LLMs. Analytical derivations confirmed empirically on actual models.
AI.conclusion: Prompt injection attacks can cause exponential growth in jailbreak success rates due to fundamental statistical physics principles, with longer prompts creating ordered phases that make attacks exponentially more effective. This provides theoretical foundation for understanding LLM vulnerabilities.

title: Gender Bias in Generative AI-assisted Recruitment Processes
summary: In recent years, generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) systems have assumed increasingly crucial roles in selection processes, personnel recruitment and analysis of candidates' profiles. However, the employment of large language models (LLMs) risks reproducing, and in some cases amplifying, gender stereotypes and bias already present in the labour market. The objective of this paper is to evaluate and measure this phenomenon, analysing how a state-of-the-art generative model (GPT-5) suggests occupations based on gender and work experience background, focusing on under-35-year-old Italian graduates. The model has been prompted to suggest jobs to 24 simulated candidate profiles, which are balanced in terms of gender, age, experience and professional field. Although no significant differences emerged in job titles and industry, gendered linguistic patterns emerged in the adjectives attributed to female and male candidates, indicating a tendency of the model to associate women with emotional and empathetic traits, while men with strategic and analytical ones. The research raises an ethical question regarding the use of these models in sensitive processes, highlighting the need for transparency and fairness in future digital labour markets.

AI.tldr: GPT-5 exhibits gendered linguistic bias when recommending jobs for Italian graduates, associating women with emotional traits and men with analytical ones, despite no significant differences in actual job suggestions.
AI.motivation: As generative AI systems become increasingly involved in hiring and selection processes, there's a growing concern that large language models may reproduce and amplify existing gender stereotypes and biases present in the labor market, particularly for sensitive decision-making processes.
AI.method: Researchers analyzed GPT-5's job suggestions for 24 simulated candidate profiles of under-35-year-old Italian graduates, with profiles balanced by gender, age, experience, and professional field. The model was prompted to suggest occupations based on gender and work experience background.
AI.result: While no significant differences emerged in job titles and industries suggested for male vs. female candidates, the model showed gendered linguistic patterns in the adjectives used - associating women with emotional and empathetic traits while linking men with strategic and analytical characteristics.
AI.conclusion: The research highlights ethical concerns about using LLMs in sensitive hiring processes and emphasizes the need for greater transparency and fairness in future digital labor markets to prevent the reinforcement of gender stereotypes.

title: Teleodynamic Learning a new Paradigm For Interpretable AI
summary: We introduce Teleodynamic Learning, a new paradigm for machine learning in which learning is not the minimization of a fixed objective, but the emergence and stabilization of functional organization under constraint. Inspired by living systems, this framework treats intelligence as the coupled evolution of three quantities: what a system can represent, how it adapts its parameters, and which changes its internal resources can sustain. We formalize learning as a constrained dynamical process with two interacting timescales: inner dynamics for continuous parameter adaptation and outer dynamics for discrete structural change, linked by an endogenous resource variable that both shapes and is shaped by the trajectory. This perspective reveals three phenomena that standard optimization does not naturally capture: self-stabilization without externally imposed stopping rules, phase-structured learning dynamics that move from under-structuring through teleodynamic growth to over-structuring, and convergence guarantees grounded in information geometry rather than convexity. We instantiate the framework in the Distinction Engine (DE11), a teleodynamic learner grounded in Spencer-Brown's Laws of Form, information geometry, and tropical optimization. On standard benchmarks, DE11 achieves 93.3 percent test accuracy on IRIS, 92.6 percent on WINE, and 94.7 percent on Breast Cancer, while producing interpretable logical rules that arise endogenously from the learning dynamics rather than being imposed by hand. More broadly, Teleodynamic Learning unifies regularization, architecture search, and resource-bounded inference within a single principle: learning as the co-evolution of structure, parameters, and resources under constraint. This opens a thermodynamically grounded route to adaptive, interpretable, and self-organizing AI.

AI.tldr: Teleodynamic Learning: A new ML paradigm where learning emerges from self-organization under constraints, unifying representation, adaptation, and resource management in a thermodynamic framework.
AI.motivation: Current machine learning treats learning as minimization of a fixed objective function, which doesn't capture how living systems naturally organize and stabilize under constraints. The authors want to develop a framework that treats intelligence as emergent organization rather than optimization.
AI.method: Formalizes learning as constrained dynamical process with two timescales: inner dynamics for continuous parameter adaptation and outer dynamics for discrete structural change, linked by endogenous resource variable. Instantiated in Distinction Engine (DE11) based on Spencer-Brown's Laws of Form, information geometry, and tropical optimization.
AI.result: DE11 achieves 93.3% accuracy on IRIS, 92.6% on WINE, and 94.7% on Breast Cancer benchmarks. Produces interpretable logical rules that emerge naturally from learning dynamics rather than being manually imposed. Demonstrates self-stabilization without external stopping rules and phase-structured learning dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Teleodynamic Learning unifies regularization, architecture search, and resource-bounded inference within a single principle of co-evolution under constraints. Provides thermodynamically grounded route to adaptive, interpretable, self-organizing AI beyond traditional optimization paradigms.

title: CINDI: Conditional Imputation and Noisy Data Integrity with Flows in Power Grid Data
summary: Real-world multivariate time series, particularly in critical infrastructure such as electrical power grids, are often corrupted by noise and anomalies that degrade the performance of downstream tasks. Standard data cleaning approaches often rely on disjoint strategies, which involve detecting errors with one model and imputing them with another. Such approaches can fail to capture the full joint distribution of the data and ignore prediction uncertainty. This work introduces Conditional Imputation and Noisy Data Integrity (CINDI), an unsupervised probabilistic framework designed to restore data integrity in complex time series. Unlike fragmented approaches, CINDI unifies anomaly detection and imputation into a single end-to-end system built on conditional normalizing flows. By modeling the exact conditional likelihood of the data, the framework identifies low-probability segments and iteratively samples statistically consistent replacements. This allows CINDI to efficiently reuse learned information while preserving the underlying physical and statistical properties of the system. We evaluate the framework using real-world grid loss data from a Norwegian power distribution operator, though the methodology is designed to generalize to any multivariate time series domain. The results demonstrate that CINDI yields robust performance compared to competitive baselines, offering a scalable solution for maintaining reliability in noisy environments.

AI.tldr: CINDI is an unsupervised probabilistic framework that unifies anomaly detection and imputation for multivariate time series using conditional normalizing flows.
AI.motivation: Real-world multivariate time series in critical infrastructure like power grids are often corrupted by noise and anomalies that degrade downstream task performance. Standard disjoint approaches (separate detection and imputation models) fail to capture joint data distribution and ignore prediction uncertainty.
AI.method: CINDI unifies anomaly detection and imputation into a single end-to-end system using conditional normalizing flows. It models exact conditional likelihood of data, identifies low-probability segments, and iteratively samples statistically consistent replacements while preserving underlying physical and statistical properties.
AI.result: CINDI demonstrates robust performance compared to competitive baselines on real-world grid loss data from a Norwegian power distribution operator. The method scales well for maintaining reliability in noisy environments and is designed to generalize to any multivariate time series domain.
AI.conclusion: CINDI provides a scalable, unified probabilistic framework for data cleaning that outperforms disjoint approaches by capturing joint data distributions and addressing uncertainty, making it suitable for critical infrastructure applications.

title: Multilingual Financial Fraud Detection Using Machine Learning and Transformer Models: A Bangla-English Study
summary: Financial fraud detection has emerged as a critical research challenge amid the rapid expansion of digital financial platforms. Although machine learning approaches have demonstrated strong performance in identifying fraudulent activities, most existing research focuses exclusively on English-language data, limiting applicability to multilingual contexts. Bangla (Bengali), despite being spoken by over 250 million people, remains largely unexplored in this domain. In this work, we investigate financial fraud detection in a multilingual Bangla-English setting using a dataset comprising legitimate and fraudulent financial messages. We evaluate classical machine learning models (Logistic Regression, Linear SVM, and Ensemble classifiers) using TF-IDF features alongside transformer-based architectures. Experimental results using 5-fold stratified cross-validation demonstrate that Linear SVM achieves the best performance with 91.59 percent accuracy and 91.30 percent F1 score, outperforming the transformer model (89.49 percent accuracy, 88.88 percent F1) by approximately 2 percentage points. The transformer exhibits higher fraud recall (94.19 percent) but suffers from elevated false positive rates. Exploratory analysis reveals distinctive patterns: scam messages are longer, contain urgency-inducing terms, and frequently include URLs (32 percent) and phone numbers (97 percent), while legitimate messages feature transactional confirmations and specific currency references. Our findings highlight that classical machine learning with well-crafted features remains competitive for multilingual fraud detection, while also underscoring the challenges posed by linguistic diversity, code-mixing, and low-resource language constraints.

AI.tldr: This paper explores financial fraud detection in a multilingual Bangla-English context, finding that classical Linear SVM with TF-IDF features outperforms transformer models despite the latter having higher fraud recall.
AI.motivation: Financial fraud detection is crucial with digital financial expansion, but most research focuses only on English, leaving major languages like Bangla (spoken by 250M people) unexplored in multilingual fraud detection contexts.
AI.method: Used a dataset of legitimate and fraudulent financial messages in Bangla-English setting. Evaluated classical ML models (Logistic Regression, Linear SVM, Ensemble classifiers) with TF-IDF features alongside transformer-based architectures, using 5-fold stratified cross-validation.
AI.result: Linear SVM achieved best performance: 91.59% accuracy and 91.30% F1 score, outperforming transformer model (89.49% accuracy, 88.88% F1). Transformer had higher fraud recall (94.19%) but suffered from elevated false positive rates. Scam messages were longer with urgency terms, URLs (32%), phone numbers (97%); legitimate messages had transactional confirmations and currency references.
AI.conclusion: Classical ML with well-crafted features remains competitive for multilingual fraud detection, but linguistic diversity, code-mixing, and low-resource language constraints pose significant challenges for the field.

title: Anomaly detection in time-series via inductive biases in the latent space of conditional normalizing flows
summary: Deep generative models for anomaly detection in multivariate time-series are typically trained by maximizing data likelihood. However, likelihood in observation space measures marginal density rather than conformity to structured temporal dynamics, and therefore can assign high probability to anomalous or out-of-distribution samples. We address this structural limitation by relocating the notion of anomaly to a prescribed latent space. We introduce explicit inductive biases in conditional normalizing flows, modeling time-series observations within a discrete-time state-space framework that constrains latent representations to evolve according to prescribed temporal dynamics. Under this formulation, expected behavior corresponds to compliance with a specified distribution over latent trajectories, while anomalies are defined as violations of these dynamics. Anomaly detection is consequently reduced to a statistically grounded compliance test, such that observations are mapped to latent space and evaluated via goodness-of-fit tests against the prescribed latent evolution. This yields a principled decision rule that remains effective even in regions of high observation likelihood. Experiments on synthetic and real-world time-series demonstrate reliable detection of anomalies in frequency, amplitude, and observation noise, while providing interpretable diagnostics of model compliance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a latent-space anomaly detection method for multivariate time-series using conditional normalizing flows with explicit temporal dynamics constraints, shifting anomaly detection from observation likelihood to latent trajectory compliance.
AI.motivation: Current deep generative models for anomaly detection rely on maximizing data likelihood in observation space, which has limitations: likelihood measures marginal density rather than structured temporal dynamics, potentially assigning high probability to anomalous or out-of-distribution samples.
AI.method: Introduces explicit inductive biases in conditional normalizing flows within a discrete-time state-space framework. The approach constrains latent representations to evolve according to prescribed temporal dynamics, modeling time-series observations in this constrained latent space.
AI.result: Experiments on synthetic and real-world time-series demonstrate reliable detection of anomalies in frequency, amplitude, and observation noise, while providing interpretable diagnostics of model compliance. The method remains effective even in regions of high observation likelihood.
AI.conclusion: By relocating anomaly detection to latent space with prescribed dynamics, the paper provides a principled decision rule grounded in statistical compliance testing, addressing structural limitations of observation-space likelihood methods while offering interpretable diagnostics.

title: abx_amr_simulator: A simulation environment for antibiotic prescribing policy optimization under antimicrobial resistance
summary: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) poses a global health threat, reducing the effectiveness of antibiotics and complicating clinical decision-making. To address this challenge, we introduce abx_amr_simulator, a Python-based simulation package designed to model antibiotic prescribing and AMR dynamics within a controlled, reinforcement learning (RL)-compatible environment. The simulator allows users to specify patient populations, antibiotic-specific AMR response curves, and reward functions that balance immedi- ate clinical benefit against long-term resistance management. Key features include a modular design for configuring patient attributes, antibiotic resistance dynamics modeled via a leaky-balloon abstraction, and tools to explore partial observability through noise, bias, and delay in observations. The package is compatible with the Gymnasium RL API, enabling users to train and test RL agents under diverse clinical scenarios. From an ML perspective, the package provides a configurable benchmark environment for sequential decision-making under uncertainty, including partial observability induced by noisy, biased, and delayed observations. By providing a customizable and extensible framework, abx_amr_simulator offers a valuable tool for studying AMR dynamics and optimizing antibiotic stewardship strategies under realistic uncertainty.

AI.tldr: A Python simulation package (abx_amr_simulator) that models antibiotic prescribing and antimicrobial resistance dynamics in a reinforcement learning-compatible environment for studying AMR and optimizing antibiotic stewardship.
AI.motivation: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) is a global health threat that reduces antibiotic effectiveness and complicates clinical decision-making, requiring tools to study and optimize antibiotic stewardship strategies.
AI.method: Created a Python-based simulation package with modular design for configuring patient populations, antibiotic-specific AMR response curves (using leaky-balloon abstraction), and reward functions. The simulator is compatible with Gymnasium RL API and allows exploration of partial observability through noise, bias, and delay in observations.
AI.result: Developed abx_amr_simulator, a configurable benchmark environment for sequential decision-making under uncertainty that enables training and testing of reinforcement learning agents under diverse clinical scenarios with realistic AMR dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The abx_amr_simulator provides a valuable customizable and extensible framework for studying AMR dynamics and optimizing antibiotic stewardship strategies under realistic uncertainty, bridging clinical needs with machine learning research.

title: Understanding Wikidata Qualifiers: An Analysis and Taxonomy
summary: This paper presents an in-depth analysis of Wikidata qualifiers, focusing on their semantics and actual usage, with the aim of developing a taxonomy that addresses the challenges of selecting appropriate qualifiers, querying the graph, and making logical inferences. The study evaluates qualifier importance based on frequency and diversity, using a modified Shannon entropy index to account for the "long tail" phenomenon. By analyzing a Wikidata dump, the top 300 qualifiers were selected and categorized into a refined taxonomy that includes contextual, epistemic/uncertainty, structural, and additional qualifiers. The taxonomy aims to guide contributors in creating and querying statements, improve qualifier recommendation systems, and enhance knowledge graph design methodologies. The results show that the taxonomy effectively covers the most important qualifiers and provides a structured approach to understanding and utilizing qualifiers in Wikidata.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes Wikidata qualifiers to develop a taxonomy addressing qualifier selection, graph querying, and logical inference challenges, using frequency/diversity metrics to identify important qualifiers and categorize them into a refined taxonomy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in selecting appropriate qualifiers, querying the Wikidata knowledge graph effectively, and making logical inferences - problems that arise due to the complex nature of qualifiers in Wikidata statements.
AI.method: The study analyzes a Wikidata dump to evaluate qualifier importance based on frequency and diversity, using a modified Shannon entropy index to account for the "long tail" phenomenon. The top 300 qualifiers were selected and categorized into a refined taxonomy through systematic analysis.
AI.result: The analysis produced a refined taxonomy with four main categories: contextual, epistemic/uncertainty, structural, and additional qualifiers. This taxonomy effectively covers the most important qualifiers and provides structured guidance for Wikidata contributors and query systems.
AI.conclusion: The developed taxonomy successfully addresses the identified challenges by providing a structured approach that can guide contributors in creating statements, improve qualifier recommendation systems, and enhance knowledge graph design methodologies for Wikidata.

title: Relaxed Efficient Acquisition of Context and Temporal Features
summary: In many biomedical applications, measurements are not freely available at inference time: each laboratory test, imaging modality, or assessment incurs financial cost, time burden, or patient risk. Longitudinal active feature acquisition (LAFA) seeks to optimize predictive performance under such constraints by adaptively selecting measurements over time, yet the problem remains inherently challenging due to temporally coupled decisions (missed early measurements cannot be revisited, and acquisition choices influence all downstream predictions). Moreover, real-world clinical workflows typically begin with an initial onboarding phase, during which relatively stable contextual descriptors (e.g., demographics or baseline characteristics) are collected once and subsequently condition longitudinal decision-making. Despite its practical importance, the efficient selection of onboarding context has not been studied jointly with temporally adaptive acquisition. We therefore propose REACT (Relaxed Efficient Acquisition of Context and Temporal features), an end-to-end differentiable framework that simultaneously optimizes (i) selection of onboarding contextual descriptors and (ii) adaptive feature--time acquisition plans for longitudinal measurements under cost constraints. REACT employs a Gumbel--Sigmoid relaxation with straight-through estimation to enable gradient-based optimization over discrete acquisition masks, allowing direct backpropagation from prediction loss and acquisition cost. Across real-world longitudinal health and behavioral datasets, REACT achieves improved predictive performance at lower acquisition costs compared to existing longitudinal acquisition baselines, demonstrating the benefit of modeling onboarding and temporally coupled acquisition within a unified optimization framework.

AI.tldr: REACT is a novel framework that jointly optimizes selection of initial onboarding context features and adaptive longitudinal feature acquisition under cost constraints using differentiable relaxation techniques.
AI.motivation: Biomedical measurements incur real costs (financial, time, patient risk) and can't always be acquired freely at inference. Current approaches don't jointly consider initial onboarding context selection with adaptive longitudinal acquisition, missing practical clinical workflow considerations.
AI.method: REACT uses an end-to-end differentiable framework with Gumbel-Sigmoid relaxation and straight-through estimation to optimize discrete acquisition masks, allowing gradient-based optimization of both onboarding context selection and temporal feature acquisition under cost constraints.
AI.result: REACT achieves improved predictive performance at lower acquisition costs compared to existing longitudinal acquisition baselines across real-world longitudinal health and behavioral datasets.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling onboarding context selection with temporally coupled acquisition in a unified optimization framework provides significant benefits for cost-constrained longitudinal prediction tasks in biomedical applications.

title: Governing Evolving Memory in LLM Agents: Risks, Mechanisms, and the Stability and Safety Governed Memory (SSGM) Framework
summary: Long-term memory has emerged as a foundational component of autonomous Large Language Model (LLM) agents, enabling continuous adaptation, lifelong multimodal learning, and sophisticated reasoning. However, as memory systems transition from static retrieval databases to dynamic, agentic mechanisms, critical concerns regarding memory governance, semantic drift, and privacy vulnerabilities have surfaced.
