We study the compute-optimal allocation of sampling compute for on-policy RL methods in LLMs, framing scaling as a compute-constrained optimization over three resources: parallel rollouts per problem, number of problems per batch, and number of update steps. We find that the compute-optimal number of parallel rollouts per problem increases predictably with compute budget and then saturates. This trend holds across both easy and hard problems, though driven by different mechanisms: solution sharpening on easy problems and coverage expansion on hard problems. We further show that increasing the number of parallel rollouts mitigates interference across problems, while the number of problems per batch primarily affects training stability and can be chosen within a broad range. Validated across base models and data distributions, our results recast RL scaling laws as prescriptive allocation rules and provide practical guidance for compute-efficient LLM RL post-training.

AI.tldr: RL post-training scaling laws show compute-optimal allocation rules for LLMs, revealing that parallel rollouts per problem increase with compute then saturate, driven by different mechanisms for easy vs hard problems.
AI.motivation: While scaling laws exist for LLM pre-training, there's poor understanding of compute-optimal allocation for reinforcement learning (RL) post-training of large language models.
AI.method: Frame scaling as compute-constrained optimization over three resources: parallel rollouts per problem, number of problems per batch, and number of update steps. Study compute-optimal allocation of sampling compute for on-policy RL methods in LLMs.
AI.result: Compute-optimal number of parallel rollouts per problem increases predictably with compute budget then saturates. This trend holds across easy and hard problems, though driven by different mechanisms: solution sharpening on easy problems and coverage expansion on hard problems. Increasing parallel rollouts mitigates interference across problems, while number of problems per batch primarily affects training stability and can be chosen broadly.
AI.conclusion: Validated across base models and data distributions, these results recast RL scaling laws as prescriptive allocation rules and provide practical guidance for compute-efficient LLM RL post-training.

title: A Quantitative Characterization of Forgetting in Post-Training
summary: Continual post-training of generative models is widely used, yet a principled understanding of when and why forgetting occurs remains limited. We develop theoretical results under a two-mode mixture abstraction (representing old and new tasks), proposed by Chen et al. (2025) (arXiv:2510.18874), and formalize forgetting in two forms: (i) mass forgetting, where the old mixture weight collapses to zero, and (ii) old-component drift, where an already-correct old component shifts during training. For equal-covariance Gaussian modes, we prove that forward-KL objectives trained on data from the new distribution drive the old weight to zero, while reverse-KL objectives converge to the true target (thereby avoiding mass forgetting) and perturb the old mean only through overlap-gated misassignment probabilities controlled by the Bhattacharyya coefficient, yielding drift that decays exponentially with mode separation and a locally well-conditioned geometry with exponential convergence. We further quantify how replay interacts with these objectives. For forward-KL, replay must modify the training distribution to change the population optimum; for reverse-KL, replay leaves the population objective unchanged but prevents finite-batch old-mode starvation through bounded importance weighting. Finally, we analyze three recently proposed near-on-policy post-training methods, SDFT (arxiv:2601.19897), TTT-Discover (arxiv:2601.16175), and OAPL (arxiv:2602.19362), via the same lens and derive explicit conditions under which each retains old mass and exhibits overlap-controlled drift. Overall, our results show that forgetting can by precisely quantified based on the interaction between divergence direction, geometric behavioral overlap, sampling regime, and the visibility of past behavior during training.

AI.tldr: Theoretical analysis shows forgetting in continual post-training depends on KL divergence direction, geometric overlap, sampling regime, and visibility of past behavior.
AI.motivation: Continual post-training of generative models faces forgetting problems, but lacks principled understanding of when and why forgetting occurs, especially in terms of theoretical mechanisms.
AI.method: Develops theoretical framework using two-mode mixture abstraction representing old and new tasks. Analyzes forward-KL vs reverse-KL objectives for equal-covariance Gaussian modes, examines replay interactions, and analyzes three recent near-on-policy methods (SDFT, TTT-Discover, OAPL).
AI.result: Forward-KL drives old weight to zero (mass forgetting), while reverse-KL avoids mass forgetting and produces old-component drift controlled by overlap-gated misassignment. Replay interacts differently with objectives: forward-KL requires modifying training distribution, reverse-KL prevents old-mode starvation via importance weighting. Three analyzed methods show conditions for retaining old mass with overlap-controlled drift.
AI.conclusion: Forgetting can be precisely quantified based on interaction between divergence direction, geometric behavioral overlap, sampling regime, and visibility of past behavior during training, providing principled understanding of continual post-training forgetting.

title: Neural Thickets: Diverse Task Experts Are Dense Around Pretrained Weights
summary: Pretraining produces a learned parameter vector that is typically treated as a starting point for further iterative adaptation. In this work, we instead view the outcome of pretraining as a distribution over parameter vectors, whose support already contains task-specific experts. We show that in small models such expert solutions occupy a negligible fraction of the volume of this distribution, making their discovery reliant on structured optimization methods such as gradient descent. In contrast, in large, well-pretrained models the density of task-experts increases dramatically, so that diverse, task-improving specialists populate a substantial fraction of the neighborhood around the pretrained weights. Motivated by this perspective, we explore a simple, fully parallel post-training method that samples $N$ parameter perturbations at random, selects the top $K$, and ensembles predictions via majority vote. Despite its simplicity, this approach is competitive with standard post-training methods such as PPO, GRPO, and ES for contemporary large-scale models.

AI.tldr: Large pretrained models contain many task-specific expert solutions in their weight neighborhoods that can be discovered through random sampling and ensembling without gradient-based optimization.
AI.motivation: Traditional view treats pretrained weights as a single starting point for gradient-based adaptation, but the authors propose viewing pretraining as creating a distribution over parameters that already contains task-specific experts, especially in large models.
AI.method: Proposes a simple parallel post-training method: sample N random parameter perturbations around pretrained weights, select top K based on task performance, and ensemble predictions via majority vote without any gradient-based optimization.
AI.result: Despite its simplicity, this random sampling and ensembling approach is competitive with sophisticated post-training methods like PPO, GRPO, and ES for contemporary large-scale models.
AI.conclusion: In large pretrained models, task-specific experts are densely distributed around the pretrained weights, making simple random sampling strategies surprisingly effective and competitive with more complex optimization-based post-training methods.

title: Cornserve: A Distributed Serving System for Any-to-Any Multimodal Models
summary:
Any-to-Any models are an emerging class of multimodal models that accept combinations of multimodal data (e.g., text, image, video, audio) as input and generate them as output. Serving these models are challenging; different requests with different input and output modalities traverse different paths through the model computation graph, and each component of the model have different scaling characteristics.
  We present Cornserve, a distributed serving system for generic Any-to-Any models. Cornserve provides a flexible task abstraction for expressing Any-to-Any model computation graphs, enabling component disaggregation and independent scaling. The distributed runtime dispatches compute to the data plane via an efficient record-and-replay execution model that keeps track of data dependencies, and forwards tensor data between components directly from the producer to the consumer. Built on Kubernetes with approximately 23K new lines of Python, Cornserve supports diverse Any-to-Any models and delivers up to 3.81$\times$ higher throughput and 5.79$\times$ lower tail latency. Cornserve is open-source, and the demo video is available on YouTube.

AI.tldr: Cornserve is a distributed serving system for Any-to-Any multimodal models that enables component disaggregation, independent scaling, and efficient data flow management to improve throughput and reduce latency.
AI.motivation: Serving Any-to-Any multimodal models is challenging because different requests with different input/output modalities follow different computation paths, and each component has different scaling characteristics.
AI.method: Provides a flexible task abstraction for expressing Any-to-Any model computation graphs, enabling component disaggregation and independent scaling. Uses a distributed runtime with an efficient record-and-replay execution model that tracks data dependencies and forwards tensor data directly between components.
AI.result: Cornserve delivers up to 3.81× higher throughput and 5.79× lower tail latency compared to existing approaches, supports diverse Any-to-Any models, and is built on Kubernetes with ~23K lines of Python code.
AI.conclusion: Cornserve effectively addresses the serving challenges of Any-to-Any multimodal models through component disaggregation, independent scaling, and efficient data flow management, making it a practical open-source solution for serving these complex models.

title: Security Considerations for Artificial Intelligence Agents
comment: Perplexity Response to NIST/CAISI Request for Information 2025-0035. 91 Fed. Reg. 698 (Jan. 8, 2026)
summary: This article, a lightly adapted version of Perplexity's response to NIST/CAISI Request for Information 2025-0035, details our observations and recommendations concerning the security of frontier AI agents. These insights are informed by Perplexity's experience operating general-purpose agentic systems used by millions of users and thousands of enterprises in both controlled and open-world environments. Agent architectures change core assumptions around code-data separation, authority boundaries, and execution predictability, creating new confidentiality, integrity, and availability failure modes. We map principal attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting boundaries, and multi-agent coordination, with particular emphasis on indirect prompt injection, confused-deputy behavior, and cascading failures in long-running workflows. We then assess current defenses as a layered stack: input-level and model-level mitigations, sandboxed execution, and deterministic policy enforcement for high-consequence actions. Finally, we identify standards and research gaps, including adaptive security benchmarks, policy models for delegation and privilege control, and guidance for secure multi-agent system design aligned with NIST risk management principles.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes security challenges in frontier AI agents, mapping attack surfaces and proposing defense strategies based on Perplexity's experience with large-scale agentic systems.
AI.motivation: AI agents introduce new security vulnerabilities due to fundamental changes in traditional computing assumptions, including blurred code-data separation, ambiguous authority boundaries, and reduced execution predictability. These create novel confidentiality, integrity, and availability risks that need systematic analysis.
AI.method: The paper uses Perplexity's operational experience with millions of users and enterprise deployments to map attack surfaces across tools, connectors, hosting boundaries, and multi-agent coordination. It analyzes specific threats like indirect prompt injection, confused-deputy behavior, and cascading failures in long-running workflows.
AI.result: The analysis identifies principal attack surfaces and assesses current defenses as a layered stack including input-level and model-level mitigations, sandboxed execution, and deterministic policy enforcement for high-consequence actions. The paper maps security weaknesses in agent architectures.
AI.conclusion: Significant research and standardization gaps exist in AI agent security, including the need for adaptive security benchmarks, policy models for delegation and privilege control, and secure multi-agent system design aligned with NIST risk management principles. The paper provides concrete recommendations for improving frontier AI agent security.

title: Separable neural architectures as a primitive for unified predictive and generative intelligence
summary: Intelligent systems across physics, language and perception often exhibit factorisable structure, yet are typically modelled by monolithic neural architectures that do not explicitly exploit this structure. The separable neural architecture (SNA) addresses this by formalising a representational class that unifies additive, quadratic and tensor-decomposed neural models. By constraining interaction order and tensor rank, SNAs impose a structural inductive bias that factorises high-dimensional mappings into low-arity components. Separability need not be a property of the system itself: it often emerges in the coordinates or representations through which the system is expressed. Crucially, this coordinate-aware formulation reveals a structural analogy between chaotic spatiotemporal dynamics and linguistic autoregression. By treating continuous physical states as smooth, separable embeddings, SNAs enable distributional modelling of chaotic systems. This approach mitigates the nonphysical drift characteristics of deterministic operators whilst remaining applicable to discrete sequences. The compositional versatility of this approach is demonstrated across four domains: autonomous waypoint navigation via reinforcement learning, inverse generation of multifunctional microstructures, distributional modelling of turbulent flow and neural language modelling. These results establish the separable neural architecture as a domain-agnostic primitive for predictive and generative intelligence, capable of unifying both deterministic and distributional representations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Separable Neural Architecture (SNA), a framework that factorizes high-dimensional mappings into low-arity components by constraining interaction order and tensor rank, unifying deterministic and distributional representations across physics, language, and perception domains.
AI.motivation: Intelligent systems across physics, language, and perception exhibit factorizable structure, but current neural architectures are monolithic and don't explicitly exploit this structural property. The authors aim to develop a framework that can capture this separability and unify diverse modeling approaches.
AI.method: The authors formalize the Separable Neural Architecture (SNA) which constrains interaction order and tensor rank to impose structural inductive bias. This factorizes high-dimensional mappings into low-arity components. The approach is "coordinate-aware," revealing that separability can emerge in the representations rather than being inherent to the system itself.
AI.result: SNAs were demonstrated across four domains: autonomous waypoint navigation via reinforcement learning, inverse generation of multifunctional microstructures, distributional modeling of turbulent flow, and neural language modeling. The approach mitigates nonphysical drift in deterministic operators while remaining applicable to discrete sequences.
AI.conclusion: The Separable Neural Architecture serves as a domain-agnostic primitive for predictive and generative intelligence, capable of unifying both deterministic and distributional representations across diverse domains from physics to language.

title: The Latent Color Subspace: Emergent Order in High-Dimensional Chaos
summary: Text-to-image generation models have advanced rapidly, yet achieving fine-grained control over generated images remains difficult, largely due to limited understanding of how semantic information is encoded. We develop an interpretation of the color representation in the Variational Autoencoder latent space of FLUX.1 [Dev], revealing a structure reflecting Hue, Saturation, and Lightness. We verify our Latent Color Subspace (LCS) interpretation by demonstrating that it can both predict and explicitly control color, introducing a fully training-free method in FLUX based solely on closed-form latent-space manipulation. Code is available at https://github.com/ExplainableML/LCS.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed a Latent Color Subspace (LCS) method that interprets and controls color in FLUX.1's VAE latent space through training-free latent-space manipulations.
AI.motivation: Text-to-image models have advanced but still lack fine-grained control over generated images, particularly due to limited understanding of how semantic information is encoded in their latent spaces.
AI.method: Developed an interpretation of color representation in FLUX.1's Variational Autoencoder latent space, revealing a structure reflecting Hue, Saturation, and Lightness. Created a fully training-free method based solely on closed-form latent-space manipulation.
AI.result: The Latent Color Subspace (LCS) interpretation can both predict and explicitly control color in FLUX.1's generated images through training-free latent-space manipulations.
AI.conclusion: The LCS method provides interpretable color control in text-to-image generation without requiring additional training, enabling fine-grained color manipulation through latent space analysis.

title: Temporal Straightening for Latent Planning
summary: Learning good representations is essential for latent planning with world models. While pretrained visual encoders produce strong semantic visual features, they are not tailored to planning and contain information irrelevant -- or even detrimental -- to planning. Inspired by the perceptual straightening hypothesis in human visual processing, we introduce temporal straightening to improve representation learning for latent planning. Using a curvature regularizer that encourages locally straightened latent trajectories, we jointly learn an encoder and a predictor. We show that reducing curvature this way makes the Euclidean distance in latent space a better proxy for the geodesic distance and improves the conditioning of the planning objective. We demonstrate empirically that temporal straightening makes gradient-based planning more stable and yields significantly higher success rates across a suite of goal-reaching tasks.

AI.tldr: Temporal straightening improves representation learning for latent planning by encouraging locally straightened latent trajectories, making Euclidean distance a better proxy for geodesic distance and improving planning stability and success rates.
AI.motivation: Pretrained visual encoders produce strong semantic features but contain information irrelevant or detrimental to planning, creating suboptimal representations for latent planning with world models.
AI.method: Introduce temporal straightening using a curvature regularizer that encourages locally straightened latent trajectories. Jointly learn an encoder and predictor while reducing curvature to improve representation geometry.
AI.result: Temporal straightening makes Euclidean distance in latent space a better proxy for geodesic distance, improves conditioning of planning objectives, makes gradient-based planning more stable, and yields significantly higher success rates across goal-reaching tasks.
AI.conclusion: Applying temporal straightening inspired by perceptual straightening in human vision improves representation learning for planning, demonstrating that reducing trajectory curvature leads to more effective latent planning.

title: STAMP: Selective Task-Aware Mechanism for Text Privacy
summary: We present STAMP (Selective Task-Aware Mechanism for Text Privacy), a new framework for task-aware text privatization that achieves an improved privacy-utility trade-off. STAMP selectively allocates privacy budgets across tokens by jointly considering (i) each token's importance to the downstream task (as measured via a task- or query-specific representation), and (ii) its privacy sensitivity (e.g., names, dates, identifiers). This token-level partitioning enables fine-grained, group-wise control over the level of noise applied to different parts of the input, balancing privacy protection with task relevance. To privatize individual token embeddings, we introduce the polar mechanism, which perturbs only the direction of embeddings on the unit sphere while preserving their magnitude. Decoding is performed via cosine nearest-neighbor search, aligning the perturbation geometry with the decoding geometry. Unlike isotropic noise mechanisms, the polar mechanism maintains semantic neighborhoods in the embedding space and better preserves downstream utility. Experimental evaluations on SQuAD, Yelp, and AG News datasets demonstrate that STAMP, when combined with the normalized polar mechanism, consistently achieves superior privacy-utility trade-offs across varying per-token privacy budgets.

AI.tldr: STAMP is a task-aware text privatization framework that selectively allocates privacy budgets across tokens based on their importance and sensitivity, using a polar mechanism that preserves semantic neighborhoods in embedding space.
AI.motivation: Current text privatization methods often apply uniform noise or privacy budgets, failing to balance privacy protection with downstream task utility. There's a need for fine-grained control that considers both token importance for tasks and privacy sensitivity.
AI.method: STAMP jointly considers token importance (via task/query-specific representations) and privacy sensitivity to selectively allocate privacy budgets. It uses a polar mechanism that perturbs only embedding directions while preserving magnitude, with decoding via cosine nearest-neighbor search.
AI.result: STAMP with normalized polar mechanism consistently achieves superior privacy-utility trade-offs on SQuAD, Yelp, and AG News datasets across varying per-token privacy budgets, outperforming isotropic noise mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware selective privacy budgeting with geometry-aware perturbation mechanisms like polar mechanism enables better privacy-utility trade-offs in text privatization by preserving semantic neighborhoods while protecting sensitive information.

title: Matching Features, Not Tokens: Energy-Based Fine-Tuning of Language Models
summary: Cross-entropy (CE) training provides dense and scalable supervision for language models, but it optimizes next-token prediction under teacher forcing rather than sequence-level behavior under model rollouts. We introduce a feature-matching objective for language-model fine-tuning that targets sequence-level statistics of the completion distribution, providing dense semantic feedback without requiring a task-specific verifier or preference model. To optimize this objective efficiently, we propose energy-based fine-tuning (EBFT), which uses strided block-parallel sampling to generate multiple rollouts from nested prefixes concurrently, batches feature extraction over these rollouts, and uses the resulting embeddings to perform an on-policy policy-gradient update. We present a theoretical perspective connecting EBFT to KL-regularized feature-matching and energy-based modeling. Empirically, across Q&A coding, unstructured coding, and translation, EBFT matches RLVR and outperforms SFT on downstream accuracy while achieving a lower validation cross-entropy than both methods.

AI.tldr: EBFT is a new language model fine-tuning method that uses feature matching at the sequence level instead of next-token cross-entropy, enabling efficient on-policy updates via strided block-parallel sampling.
AI.motivation: Cross-entropy training only optimizes next-token prediction under teacher forcing, not sequence-level behavior under model rollouts, which limits performance on downstream tasks.
AI.method: EBFT uses a feature-matching objective targeting sequence-level statistics, optimized through strided block-parallel sampling to generate multiple rollouts from nested prefixes concurrently, batching feature extraction, and performing on-policy policy-gradient updates.
AI.result: EBFT matches RLVR and outperforms supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on downstream accuracy across Q&A coding, unstructured coding, and translation tasks, while achieving lower validation cross-entropy than both methods.
AI.conclusion: EBFT provides an effective alternative to CE training that optimizes sequence-level behavior directly, demonstrating better downstream performance and theoretical connections to KL-regularized feature matching and energy-based modeling.
